---
title: "Collaboration and Support"
---

🚦🚧 This section is under construction 🚧🚦

<!-- ## Collaboration is part of reproducibility

Version control is not only for backup. It is how the lab understands change, reviews methods, and avoids conflicting local copies of the same project.

The presentation frames Git and GitHub as the tool for traceability, and that is exactly right: commit history should explain what changed, when, and why.

## Daily Git habits

Use Git for active work, not just for milestone dumps.

- Create the repository early.
- Commit small, coherent changes.
- Use commit messages that describe the decision or milestone.
- Push regularly so the work is visible and recoverable.
- Keep data out of Git unless policy clearly allows it.

The NSAPH handbook also recommends pushing code regularly, especially before asking for help or presenting work.[^nsaph-github]

## Branches and pull requests

For solo projects, a lightweight branch workflow is still useful:

1. Make a branch for risky or substantial work.
2. Open a pull request when the method or output changes meaningfully.
3. Use review comments to discuss code, assumptions, and documentation gaps.
4. Merge only when the branch leaves the repository in a reproducible state.

## What a good commit message looks like

Better:

- `add first pass of temperature exposure merge`
- `document MCBS variable cleaning assumptions`
- `move repeated model code into helper function`

Worse:

- `updates`
- `stuff`
- `final final version`

## How to ask for help effectively

R for Data Science recommends preparing a minimal reproducible example when you are stuck.[^r4ds-help] That is one of the highest-leverage habits students can learn.

When asking the RSE or another lab member for help, include:

1. the repo or branch,
2. the exact script or notebook,
3. the command you ran,
4. the full error message,
5. the smallest reproducible example you can produce,
6. and any data access constraints that prevent direct debugging.

## What to send before a meeting

For technical meetings, send:

- the current notebook or report,
- a short status note,
- open questions,
- and links to the relevant code or pull request.

This shifts meetings away from "what happened?" and toward "what decision do we need to make?"

## Code review checklist

Review for:

- reproducibility,
- path portability,
- data leakage risk,
- hidden assumptions,
- duplicated code,
- and missing documentation.

Do not review only for style.

## Support ladder

Use this order when possible:

1. Check the project `README` and this site.
2. Search the official docs for the tool you are using.
3. Reduce the problem to a minimal example.
4. Open an issue or send a focused help request.
5. Escalate to synchronous help when the blocker persists.

## The real goal

The goal is not to make every student a Git expert. The goal is to make the lab's work inspectable, shareable, and recoverable.

## Further reading

- [R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: getting help](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/workflow-help.html)
- [NSAPH Handbook: Introduction to NSAPH GitHub](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/analysis.html)
- [NSAPH Handbook: Workflow Documentation](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/docs.html)

[^nsaph-github]: NSAPH Handbook, [*Introduction to NSAPH GitHub*](https://nsaph.github.io/handbook/analysis.html).
[^r4ds-help]: Hadley Wickham, Mine Cetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund, [*R for Data Science (2e): Workflow: getting help*](https://r4ds.hadley.nz/workflow-help.html). -->
